does: This paper identifies and addresses the problem of dimensional heterogeneity in multidimensional video reward models, where training samples vary in reliability across different evaluation dimensions. The authors propose a disentangled influence framework that efficiently estimates dimension-specific supervision risk for each training sample. They introduce two dimension-disentangled refinement strategies that leverage these risk estimates to selectively curate or weight training data, improving how text-to-video models are evaluated across multiple axes like visual quality, temporal coherence, and text alignment. The framework moves beyond global scalar filtering to enable fine-grained, per-dimension sample curation.
connects: This work builds on recent advances in multidimensional reward models for text-to-video generation, which decompose evaluation into interpretable dimensions rather than collapsed scalar scores. It extends prior data-centric approaches like influence functions and sample filtering methods, but identifies their fundamental limitation when applied to multidimensional settings where sample quality is not uniform across objectives. The paper connects to broader research on training data quality and curation in machine learning, as well as influence function literature that typically assumes homogeneous sample importance. It also relates to work on disentangled representations and multi-task learning where different objectives can have conflicting gradients.
enables: This framework enables more robust training of video reward models by better utilizing supervision signals from imperfect data. Future work can apply disentangled influence functions to other multidimensional evaluation problems in vision and multimodal learning. The approach opens paths for developing video models that better align with the multifaceted nature of human preference, potentially improving text-to-video quality metrics and generation. More broadly, the dimensional heterogeneity phenomenon and its solutions could inform data curation strategies across multi-objective machine learning tasks, enabling more principled approaches to handling conflicting training signals in complex domains.
